title: Dimensionality of a strongly interacting 2D-3D Fermi-Fermi mixture from the perspective of superfluid instability and excitation properties
authors: Haruka Takeda, Saki Hirai, Shumpei Iwasaki, Yoji Ohashi
abstract: We theoretically investigate strong-coupling properties of an attractively interacting Fermi atomic gas, where the Cooper-pair formation occurs between atoms belonging to different dimensional bands. Including pairing fluctuations within the framework of the self-consistent $T$-matrix approximation (SCTMA), we examine how the BCS-type superfluid phase transition temperature $T_\mathrm{c}$ varies as one moves from the 3D-3D to the 2D-3D system, in the wide parameter region with respect to the strength of the pairing interaction. In the 2D-3D limit, we find that, while the mean-field BCS theory predicts $T_\mathrm{c}>0$ in the strong-coupling regime, $T_\mathrm{c}$ is remarkably suppressed down to zero by pairing fluctuations that are strongly enhanced by the mixed-dimensionality of the system. As the origin of this, we clarify that the lower-dimensional (2D) component dominates the superfluid instability, so that the vanishing $T_\mathrm{c}$ is the same phenomenon as that in the 2D-2D case. We also point out that this can already be seen in the mean-field level, when one examines the propagation of the Goldstone mode. On the other hand, we find that the pseudogap phenomenon, which is known as a precursor of Cooper-pair formation, exhibits a 3D character of the 2D-3D system. These results indicate that the dimensionality of a strongly interacting Fermi gas depends on what we observe.
summary: This theoretical study examines how superfluidity behaves in a Fermi gas whose dimensionality is tuned between 3D-3D and 2D-3D. Using advanced many-body techniques, the authors find that pairing fluctuations suppress the critical temperature to zero in the 2D-3D limit. Crucially, they show that while the superfluid instability is dominated by the 2D component, the precursor pseudogap retains a 3D character, highlighting the observable dependence on dimensionality.
main_problem: The study investigates the strong-coupling properties and superfluid instability of an attractively interacting Fermi-Fermi mixture as its dimensionality is tuned from 3D-3D to 2D-3D.
main_result: Pairing fluctuations suppress the superfluid transition temperature ($T_c$) to zero in the 2D-3D limit, which is governed by the lower-dimensional component. However, the pseudogap phenomenon retains a 3D character.
method: The analysis employs the Self-Consistent T-matrix Approximation (SCTMA) and Generalized Random Phase Approximation (GRPA) to calculate superfluid properties and excitation spectra.
model_or_system: The system is a two-component Fermi atomic gas with tunable s-wave pairing interaction, loaded in a mixed-dimensionality trap (2D in-plane, 3D along z).
key_observables: Superfluid transition temperature ($T_c$), superfluid order parameter ($\Delta$), Goldstone mode velocity ($\mathbf{v}_\varphi$), and single-particle spectral weight ($A_\sigma$).
important_parameters: Interaction strength ($U$), dimensional imbalance (2D-3D limit), and the pairing interaction strength.
main_assumptions: The use of SCTMA is crucial for including pairing fluctuations, and the analysis relies on the Thouless criterion for determining $T_c$.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the $T_c$ dependence, the vanishing $v_{\phi,z}$ component of the Goldstone mode velocity in the 2D-3D limit, and the pseudogap structure in the single-particle density of states.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the theoretical framework using SCTMA, then analyzing the superfluid instability via $T_c$ calculations, and finally examining the single-particle excitation spectrum, particularly the pseudogap, in the mixed-dimensional limit.
why_interesting: This work provides detailed insights into how dimensionality fundamentally alters superfluidity and pairing mechanisms in ultracold atomic gases, distinguishing between mean-field predictions and fluctuation-dominated regimes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Mean-ﬁeld BCS results for (a) superﬂuid order pa- rameter ∆at T = 0, and (b) superﬂuid phase transition tem- perature Tc, in a two-component 2D-3D Fermi-Fermi mixture with an s-wave pairing interaction. ǫF = k2 F/(2m) and kF is given in Eq. (5), where m is an atomic mass. aeﬀf is the eﬀective s-wave scattering length in Eq. (4). We have used the mean-ﬁeld theory in Ref. [31] to obtain these results, and the outline of this theory is explained in Appendix A.

FIG. 3. (a) SCTMA self-energy Σσ(p, iωm) in Eq. (7). The double solid line is the dressed Green’s function Gσ in Eq. (6). (b) Scattering matrix Γ(q, iνn) in Eq. (8). The wavy line de- scribes the paring interaction −U. In this ﬁgure, pseudospin −σ represents the opposite spin component to σ = ↑, ↓.

FIG. 2. Diﬀerence of Fermi surfaces between spin-↑(dashed line) and spin-↓(solid line) components in a non-interacting mixed-dimensional Fermi gas with the kinetic energy in Eq. (3). p⊥and pz denote the momentum in the in-plane direction and the inter-layer direction, respectively. We set N↑= N↓= 1/(6π2d3 z).

FIG. 4. Calculated BCS-type superﬂuid phase transition temperature Tc in SCTMA, as a function of the interaction strength (kFaeﬀ)−1 and the hopping parameter t↑. In this ﬁg- ure, 3D-3D and 2D-3D cases are, respectively, realized when t↑/ǫF = 1 and 0.

FIG. 5. Comparison of the calculated Tc in SCTMA with TBEC obtained from Eq. (23). (a) Tc and TBEC as functions of t↑/ǫF in the strong-coupling regime when (kFaeﬀ)−1 = 6. (b) Interaction dependence in the 3D-3D case (t↑/ǫF = t↓/ǫF = 1).
